message:
chore(beads): sync issues.jsonl with latest bead state

Add three new bead entries reflecting recently created/updated tasks
in the issue tracker. These entries were generated by concurrent agent
sessions and capture the current dependency graph and status metadata
for downstream bead tooling (br/bv).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-5lbr","title":"[Task] Opt 5.2: Add tests for wildcard regex caching","description":"# Task: Add Tests for Wildcard Regex Caching

## Objective

Create tests that verify regex caching produces identical results and improves performance.

## Test Strategy

### 1. Equivalence Test
```rust
#[test]
fn regex_cache_same_results() {
    let index = create_test_index();
    let query = "*error*";
    
    // First query (cache miss)
    let results1 = search_wildcard(&index, query);
    
    // Second query (cache hit)
    let results2 = search_wildcard(&index, query);
    
    assert_eq!(results1.hits.len(), results2.hits.len());
    for (r1, r2) in results1.hits.iter().zip(&results2.hits) {
        assert_eq!(r1.message_id, r2.message_id);
    }
}
```

### 2. Cache Hit Verification
```rust
#[test]
fn regex_cache_hits_tracked() {
    // Clear cache
    REGEX_CACHE.lock().unwrap().clear();
    
    let query = "*test*";
    
    // First query - should be cache miss
    let _ = search_wildcard(&index, query);
    assert_eq!(get_cache_hits(), 0);
    
    // Second query - should be cache hit
    let _ = search_wildcard(&index, query);
    assert_eq!(get_cache_hits(), 1);
}
```

### 3. Cache Key Correctness
```rust
#[test]
fn regex_cache_different_fields() {
    // Same pattern, different fields = different cache entries
    let pattern = "*foo*";
    
    let q1 = build_regex_query("content", pattern);
    let q2 = build_regex_query("title", pattern);
    
    // Should be different cache entries
    assert_ne!(
        cache_key("content", pattern),
        cache_key("title", pattern)
    );
}
```

### 4. Rollback Test
```rust
#[test]
fn regex_cache_rollback() {
    // With cache
    env::remove_var("CASS_REGEX_CACHE");
    let results_cached = search_wildcard(&index, "*test*");
    
    // Without cache
    env::set_var("CASS_REGEX_CACHE", "0");
    let results_uncached = search_wildcard(&index, "*test*");
    
    env::remove_var("CASS_REGEX_CACHE");
    
    assert_eq!(results_cached.len(), results_uncached.len());
}
```

### 5. LRU Eviction Test
```rust
#[test]
fn regex_cache_lru_eviction() {
    // Fill cache beyond capacity
    for i in 0..100 {
        let pattern = format!("*pattern{}*", i);
        let _ = search_wildcard(&index, &pattern);
    }
    
    // Cache should have evicted old entries
    let cache_size = REGEX_CACHE.lock().unwrap().len();
    assert!(cache_size <= 64, "Cache exceeded max size: {}", cache_size);
}
```

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Equivalence test passes
- [ ] Cache hit tracking works
- [ ] Different fields = different cache entries
- [ ] Rollback via env var works
- [ ] LRU eviction works correctly
- [ ] Tests run in < 10 seconds","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-10T03:19:05.950806052Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-10T03:40:05.412395731Z","closed_at":"2026-01-10T03:40:05.412395731Z","close_reason":"Duplicates - consolidated into in2e/52sd/ktvx/yz74 chain","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-5p55","title":"[P2] Opt 6: Streaming Canonicalization","description":"## Overview
Reduce canonicalization from ~951µs to ~300µs by eliminating intermediate String allocations.

## Background (from PLAN Section 4.3 and 8)
Current `canonicalize_for_embedding()` in `canonicalize.rs:80-95`:
```rust
pub fn canonicalize_for_embedding(text: &str) -> String {
    let normalized: String = text.nfc().collect();  // Allocation 1
    let stripped = strip_markdown_and_code(&normalized);  // Allocation 2
    let whitespace_normalized = normalize_whitespace(&stripped);  // Allocation 3
    let filtered = filter_low_signal(&whitespace_normalized);  // Allocation 4
    truncate_to_chars(&filtered, MAX_EMBED_CHARS)
}
```

**Problem**: 4+ String allocations per call. Only impacts index-time and semantic query embedding, not lexical search.

## Implementation Strategy
Single-pass with buffer reuse:
```rust
pub fn canonicalize_for_embedding_streaming(text: &str) -> String {
    let mut result = String::with_capacity(text.len().min(MAX_EMBED_CHARS + 100));
    let normalized: String = text.nfc().collect();  // NFC requires look-ahead, unavoidable

    let mut in_code_block = false;
    let mut code_lines: Vec<&str> = Vec::new();
    let mut lang = String::new();

    for line in normalized.lines() {
        // Process with state machine, append directly to result
        // Avoid intermediate String allocations
    }

    result.truncate(MAX_EMBED_CHARS);
    result
}
```

## Technical Notes
- NFC normalization requires full string collection (look-ahead for combining characters), so one allocation remains unavoidable
- Savings come from eliminating intermediate `strip_markdown`, `normalize_whitespace`, and `filter_low_signal` allocations
- State machine for code block detection, whitespace normalization, low-signal filtering

## Equivalence Oracle
- Byte-for-byte identical output: `content_hash(canonicalize(text)) == content_hash(canonicalize_optimized(text))`
- Property-based: ∀ text: content_hash(old(text)) == content_hash(new(text))

## Rollback
Env var `CASS_STREAMING_CANONICALIZE=0` to use original implementation.

## Expected Impact
- 951µs → ~300µs (3x speedup)
- Only affects index-time, not query-time
- Reduces allocation pressure during bulk indexing

## Code Location
- `src/search/canonicalize.rs:80-95`

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-8q8f","title":"Canonicalization pipeline","description":"## Purpose
Implement text canonicalization for consistent embedding input quality.

## Background
Raw agent logs contain noise that hurts embedding quality:
- Markdown formatting (**bold**, [links](url))
- Huge code blocks with repetitive patterns
- Tool call transcripts, progress indicators

Canonicalization produces clean, consistent text. CRITICAL: Must be deterministic for content hashing!

## Algorithm
1. **Unicode normalize (NFC)** ← CRITICAL: ensures visually identical strings hash identically
2. Strip markdown formatting (keep text content)
3. Collapse code blocks: first 20 + last 10 lines, [code omitted] in middle
4. Normalize whitespace (collapse runs, trim)
5. Filter low-signal content ("OK", "Done.")
6. Truncate to MAX_EMBED_CHARS (default 2000)

## Why Unicode Normalization Matters
Without NFC normalization:
- "café" (4 chars, precomposed) ≠ "café" (5 chars, e + combining accent)
- These look identical but produce different hashes
- Would cause duplicate embeddings or missed incremental updates

## Config
- CASS_SEM_MAX_CHARS=2000
- CASS_SEM_CODE_HEAD_LINES=20
- CASS_SEM_CODE_TAIL_LINES=10

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Unicode NFC normalization applied first
- [ ] canonicalize_for_embedding(raw) -> String
- [ ] content_hash(raw) uses canonical text
- [ ] Deterministic (same visual input = same output)
- [ ] Handles edge cases (empty, all-code, unicode combining chars)
- [ ] Test: "café" (precomposed) == "café" (decomposed) after canonicalization

## Depends On
- sem.emb.trait

## References
- Plan: Section 4.6 Canonicalization","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-19T01:23:10.741631Z","updated_at":"2026-01-05T22:59:36.430912919Z","closed_at":"2026-01-05T16:04:26.928Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-8q8f","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-vmet","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:00:37Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-8uw2","title":"[BASELINE] Record Performance Baselines Before Optimization","description":"## Overview (from PLAN Section 1.A and Section 2)

Before implementing ANY optimization, we must record comprehensive baseline metrics. This ensures we can accurately measure improvement and detect regressions.

## Required Baseline Measurements

### 1. Benchmark Suite (Section 2.1)
Run and record these benchmarks:
```bash
cargo bench --bench search_perf -- --save-baseline main
cargo bench --bench index_perf -- --save-baseline main
cargo bench --bench runtime_perf -- --save-baseline main
```

**Key Metrics to Record:**
| Benchmark | Current p50 | Target |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| `vector_index_search_50k` | 56.1 ms | 2-3 ms |
| `vector_index_search_10k` | 11.2 ms | <1 ms |
| `search_latency` (40 convs) | 10.5 µs | <5 µs |
| `wildcard_large_dataset/substring` | 7.5 ms | <2 ms |
| `canonicalize_long_message` | 951 µs | ~300 µs |
| `index_small_batch` | 13.3 ms | maintain |

### 2. Indexing Metrics (Section 2.2)
For a corpus of 3000 conversations × 12 messages = 36,000 messages:
- `elapsed_ms`: p50/p95/p99
- wall clock time
- throughput (messages/s)
- peak RSS (MB)

```bash
# Generate profiling corpus
cargo run --release -- index --full 2>&1 | tee /tmp/index_baseline.log
```

### 3. Search Latency by Query Type (Section 2.3)
Run N=200 iterations for each query type:
| Query Type | p50 | p95 | p99 |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|
| exact (`serialize`) | 3ms | 4ms | 4ms |
| prefix (`ser*`) | 3ms | 3ms | 4ms |
| suffix (`*ialize`) | 6ms | 7ms | 7ms |
| substring (`*erial*`) | 9ms | 10ms | 10ms |
| phrase (`"serialize benchmark"`) | 3ms | 4ms | 4ms |

**IMPORTANT**: CLI-per-search includes cold-open costs. Need to separate `open_ms` vs `query_ms`.

## Recording Location

Store baseline results in:
- Git tag: `perf-baseline-round1`
- Criterion baselines: `target/criterion/*/main/`
- Manual measurements: `docs/perf/baseline_round1.md`

## When to Run

1. **Before starting ANY P0 optimization**
2. **After each optimization** (to create new baseline)
3. **Before creating a PR** (to include before/after comparison)

## Dependencies
- Must complete BEFORE any optimization implementation begins

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-9210b91f","title":"Dry-Run Mode (--dry-run)","description":"# Dry-Run Mode (--dry-run)

## Problem Statement
Agents want to validate queries without executing them:
- Is the syntax valid?
- How will it be interpreted?
- What would the cost be?

Currently, the only way to check is to run the query and examine results.

## Proposed Solution
Add `--dry-run` flag that validates and analyzes without executing:
```bash
cass search "complex query" --dry-run --json
```

Output:
```json
{
  "dry_run": true,
  "valid": true,
  "explanation": {...},
  "estimated_results": "10-100",
  "estimated_cost_ms": "<50",
  "warnings": []
}
```

## Design Decisions

### What Dry-Run Does
1. Parse the query (full validation)
2. Analyze filters and constraints
3. Estimate result count (if possible)
4. Estimate execution time
5. Return without actually searching

### What Dry-Run Does NOT Do
- Access the search index
- Return actual results
- Modify any state

### Estimation Accuracy
Result count estimation could use:
- Index statistics (total documents, term frequencies)
- Historical query patterns
- Simple heuristics

Don't over-engineer; rough estimates are valuable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `--dry-run` returns without executing search
- [ ] Invalid queries return `valid: false` with error details
- [ ] Valid queries include explanation
- [ ] Response time is <10ms (no index access)
- [ ] Works with all query types

## Effort Estimate
Low-Medium - 2-3 hours. Query parsing is already done; this skips execution.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-11-30T23:54:08.438234185Z","updated_at":"2025-12-15T06:23:15.005415254Z","closed_at":"2025-12-02T05:06:06.678765Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-94f61ee9","title":"Status Command (cass status)","description":"# Status Command (cass status)

## Problem Statement
Agents need a quick way to check system health before running searches:
- Is the index fresh?
- How many sessions are indexed?
- Are there pending updates?

Currently requires running `cass diag` which is verbose and slow.

## Proposed Solution
Add lightweight `cass status` command:
```bash
cass status --json
```

Output:
```json
{
  "healthy": true,
  "index": {
    "fresh": false,
    "last_indexed_at": "2025-01-15T10:00:00Z",
    "age_seconds": 3600,
    "stale": true,
    "stale_threshold_seconds": 1800
  },
  "database": {
    "conversations": 150,
    "messages": 4500
  },
  "pending": {
    "new_sessions": 5,
    "modified_sessions": 2,
    "connectors_with_updates": ["claude", "codex"]
  },
  "cache": {
    "entries": 47,
    "hit_rate": 0.85
  },
  "recommended_action": "run 'cass index' to update 7 sessions"
}
```

## Design Decisions

### Staleness Definition
Index is "stale" if:
- `last_indexed_at` is older than threshold (default 30 minutes)
- There are pending sessions detected via watch_state

### Performance
Status should be FAST (<100ms):
- Read watch_state.json for pending detection
- Read meta table for last_indexed_at
- Read cache stats from memory
- NO index scanning

### Recommended Action
Include human/agent-readable suggestion based on state.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `cass status --json` returns health summary
- [ ] Response time <100ms
- [ ] `fresh` accurately reflects index state
- [ ] `pending` shows sessions awaiting indexing
- [ ] `recommended_action` provides actionable guidance
- [ ] Works without --json for human-readable output

## Effort Estimate
Medium - 3-4 hours. Requires aggregating state from multiple sources.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-11-30T23:54:08.438234185Z","updated_at":"2025-12-01T02:06:28.469087590Z","closed_at":"2025-12-01T02:06:28.469087590Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-94pe","title":"Model management system (download, verify, state)","description":"## Purpose
Complete model management: manifest, state machine, download, verification, upgrades.

## Model Manifest (models.manifest.toml)
```toml
[[models]]
id = "all-minilm-l6-v2"
repo = "sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2"
revision = "e4ce9877abf3edfe10b0d82785e83bdcb973e22e"  # Pinned!
files = [
    { name = "model.onnx", sha256 = "...", size = 22713856 },
    { name = "tokenizer.json", sha256 = "...", size = 711396 },
    { name = "config.json", sha256 = "...", size = 612 },
]
license = "Apache-2.0"

# For future version updates
[models.upgrade]
check_url = "https://api.github.com/repos/.../releases/latest"  # Optional
notify_only = true  # Don't auto-upgrade, just notify
```

## State Machine
```rust
pub enum ModelState {
    NotInstalled,
    NeedsConsent,
    Downloading { progress_pct: u8, bytes: u64, total: u64 },
    Verifying,
    Ready,
    Disabled { reason: String },
    VerificationFailed { reason: String },
    UpdateAvailable { current: String, latest: String }, // ← ADDED
}
```

## Model Version Upgrades
When we ship a new model version (e.g., better model, security fix):
1. Update models.manifest.toml in new cass release
2. On startup, compare installed model revision vs manifest
3. If mismatch: set state to UpdateAvailable (notify only, don't auto-upgrade)
4. User runs `cass models install --upgrade` to update
5. After model update, vector index is automatically rebuilt

Why notify-only? Auto-upgrading could:
- Use bandwidth unexpectedly
- Break workflows if new model has different behavior
- Cause confusion if results change

## Download System
- Resumable (HTTP Range header)
- Progress reporting via channel
- Exponential backoff (3 retries, 5s → 15s → 45s)
- Timeout: 5 minutes per file
- Clean up partial files on cancel/failure

## Verification + Atomic Install
- Download to models/<name>.downloading/
- Verify SHA256 for each file
- Atomic rename to models/<name>/
- Write .verified marker with timestamp + revision

## Index Migration on Model Change
When model revision changes:
1. Detect: installed model revision != index metadata embedder_revision
2. Mark semantic as unavailable temporarily
3. Rebuild vector index in background
4. Notify user: "Rebuilding semantic index for new model..."
5. When complete, semantic becomes available

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Full download → verify → install flow
- [ ] Partial download resumes correctly
- [ ] Corrupt download detected and retried
- [ ] State transitions are correct
- [ ] No network without explicit consent
- [ ] Model version mismatch detected on startup
- [ ] Index rebuild triggered after model upgrade
- [ ] Cancel download cleans up partial files

## Depends On
- sem.emb.ml (FastEmbed embedder)

## References
- Plan: Section 3 (Network Policy, Consent, Model Management)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"OpusAgent","created_at":"2025-12-19T01:24:44.625634Z","updated_at":"2026-01-05T22:59:36.432582293Z","closed_at":"2026-01-05T16:00:19.325730Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-94pe","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-mwsa","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:00:43Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-u07k","title":"[Task] Opt 3.1: Verify VectorRow is Send+Sync for Rayon","description":"# Task: Verify VectorRow is Send+Sync for Rayon

## Objective

Before implementing parallel search with Rayon, verify that `VectorRow` and related types implement `Send` and `Sync` traits, which are required for safe parallel iteration.

## Investigation Steps

### 1. Check VectorRow Definition
```bash
# Find VectorRow definition
rg "struct VectorRow" src/
```

### 2. Verify Trait Implementations
```rust
// Add to tests or run in playground
fn assert_send<T: Send>() {}
fn assert_sync<T: Sync>() {}

#[test]
fn vector_row_is_send_sync() {
    assert_send::<VectorRow>();
    assert_sync::<VectorRow>();
}
```

### 3. Check All Fields
VectorRow should only contain:
- Primitive types (u64, u32, f32, etc.) - inherently Send+Sync
- &str/String - Send+Sync
- No Rc, RefCell, raw pointers

### 4. Check VectorIndex Sharability
For parallel search, we need:
```rust
// VectorIndex must be sharable across threads
fn assert_sync<T: Sync>() {}
assert_sync::<VectorIndex>();  // Or &VectorIndex must be Send
```

## Expected Findings

VectorRow likely contains only:
- `message_id: u64`
- `chunk_idx: u32`
- `vec_offset: usize`

All primitive types = Send + Sync ✓

## Potential Issues

If VectorRow contains:
- `Rc<T>` → Not Send, need to use `Arc<T>`
- `RefCell<T>` → Not Sync, need different design
- Raw pointer → May need unsafe impl or wrapper

## Document Findings

1. List all fields in VectorRow
2. Confirm Send+Sync status
3. Note any required changes

## Validation Checklist

- [ ] VectorRow definition found
- [ ] All fields enumerated
- [ ] Send+Sync compile test passes
- [ ] VectorIndex sharability confirmed
- [ ] No blocking issues identified

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-u0cv","title":"Advanced Performance Optimizations Round 1 (OPUS Analysis)","description":"# Performance Optimization Epic

## Background
A deep, ultra-intensive code analysis was performed to identify provably-isomorphic optimizations
in the cass codebase. These optimizations maintain identical output while improving performance
through better algorithms, data structures, and memory access patterns.

## Scope
18 distinct optimization opportunities identified across 4 tiers:
- **Tier 1 (High Impact):** 5 optimizations with 15-60% improvement potential
- **Tier 2 (Medium Impact):** 5 optimizations with 5-20% improvement potential  
- **Tier 3 (Architectural):** 3 optimizations requiring structural changes
- **Tier 4 (Micro-optimizations):** 5 small targeted improvements

## Key Principles
1. **Isomorphic Changes Only:** All optimizations produce identical outputs
2. **Measurable Impact:** Each has benchmarkable before/after metrics
3. **No Over-Engineering:** Minimum complexity for maximum gain
4. **Hot Path Focus:** Changes target actual performance-critical code paths

## Files Analyzed
- src/search/vector_index.rs (CVVI format, SIMD dot products)
- src/search/query.rs (query execution, caching, RRF fusion)
- src/search/tantivy.rs (edge n-gram generation)
- src/search/canonicalize.rs (text normalization)
- src/storage/sqlite.rs (metadata parsing, schema)
- src/connectors/mod.rs (workspace path matching)
- src/indexer/mod.rs (parallel scanning, agent discovery)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-12T05:48:16.840196514Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-12T17:30:57.845533911Z","closed_at":"2026-01-12T17:30:57.845533911Z","close_reason":"OPUS analysis complete: 18 optimizations identified across 4 tiers.  {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-u4s7","title":"P1.3a: Dual FTS5 Strategy (Code vs Prose)","description":"# P1.3a: Dual FTS5 Strategy (Code vs Prose)

**Parent Phase:** Phase 1: Core Export
**Section Reference:** Plan Document Section 9.2, lines 1623-1658
**Depends On:** P1.3 (FTS5 Index Generation)

## Goal

Implement dual FTS5 indexes optimized for different content types: one for natural language prose (with Porter stemming) and one for code/paths (with unicode tokenization).

## Why Two Indexes?

| Content Type | Best Tokenizer | Example Query | Reason |
|--------------|----------------|---------------|--------|
| Prose/docs | porter unicode61 | "running tests" matches "run test" | Stemming finds word variants |
| Code/paths | unicode61 tokenchars | "getUserId" exact match | Camel case, no stemming |

## Schema

```sql
-- Index 1: Prose search (Porter stemmer for natural language)
CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE messages_fts USING fts5(
    content,
    title,
    tokenize = 'porter unicode61 remove_diacritics 2'
);

-- Index 2: Code/path search (unicode with extended tokenchars)
CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE messages_code_fts USING fts5(
    content,
    source_path,
    tokenize = "unicode61 tokenchars '-_./\\:@#$%'"
);

-- Populate both indexes
INSERT INTO messages_fts(rowid, content, title)
SELECT id, content, title FROM messages;

INSERT INTO messages_code_fts(rowid, content, source_path)
SELECT m.id, m.content, c.source_path
FROM messages m
JOIN conversations c ON m.conversation_id = c.id;
```

## Query Strategy Selection

### Auto-Detection Heuristics

```javascript
function detectQueryType(query) {
    // Code patterns
    const codePatterns = [
        /[A-Z][a-z]+[A-Z]/,      // camelCase
        /[a-z]+_[a-z]+/,         // snake_case
        /\.[a-z]{2,4}$/,         // file extensions
        /\/[a-z]+\//,            // path segments
        /\.[a-zA-Z]+\(/,         // method calls
        /^(def|fn|func|class|const|let|var)\s/, // keywords
    ];
    
    const isCode = codePatterns.some(p => p.test(query));
    
    // Prose indicators
    const proseIndicators = [
        query.split(' ').length > 3,  // Multi-word
        /^(how|what|why|when|where)\b/i.test(query),  // Questions
        /\b(the|is|are|was|were)\b/i.test(query),  // Articles
    ];
    
    const isProse = proseIndicators.some(Boolean);
    
    if (isCode && !isProse) return 'code';
    if (isProse && !isCode) return 'prose';
    return 'both';  // Search both, merge results
}
```

### Search Execution

```javascript
async function search(db, query, limit = 50) {
    const queryType = detectQueryType(query);
    const sanitizedQuery = sanitizeFtsQuery(query);
    
    if (queryType === 'prose') {
        return db.exec(`
            SELECT m.*, messages_fts.rank
            FROM messages_fts
            JOIN messages m ON messages_fts.rowid = m.id
            WHERE messages_fts MATCH ?
            ORDER BY rank
            LIMIT ?
        `, [sanitizedQuery, limit]);
    }
    
    if (queryType === 'code') {
        return db.exec(`
            SELECT m.*, messages_code_fts.rank
            FROM messages_code_fts
            JOIN messages m ON messages_code_fts.rowid = m.id
            WHERE messages_code_fts MATCH ?
            ORDER BY rank
            LIMIT ?
        `, [sanitizedQuery, limit]);
    }
    
    // Search both, merge and deduplicate
    const [proseResults, codeResults] = await Promise.all([
        searchProse(db, sanitizedQuery, limit),
        searchCode(db, sanitizedQuery, limit),
    ]);
    
    return mergeAndRank(proseResults, codeResults, limit);
}
```

### Query Sanitization (FTS5 Injection Prevention)

```javascript
function sanitizeFtsQuery(query) {
    // Escape FTS5 special characters
    return query
        .replace(/"/g, '""')     // Escape quotes
        .replace(/\*/g, '')        // Remove wildcards (or allow?)
        .replace(/\^/g, '')        // Remove prefix operator
        .trim();
}

// Wrap in quotes for exact phrase
function exactPhrase(query) {
    return '"' + sanitizeFtsQuery(query) + '"';
}
```

## UI Integration

```html
<div class="search-mode">
    <button class="active" data-mode="auto">Auto</button>
    <button data-mode="prose">Prose</button>
    <button data-mode="code">Code</button>
</div>
```

```
🔍 Searching code index... (detected "getUserId" as code pattern)
```

2. "getUserId" → code index, exact match
3. "src/main.rs" → code index, path match
4. "how does auth work" → prose index

## Exit Criteria

3. Manual override available in UI
4. Results properly merged and ranked

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-ug6i","title":"[P3] Opt 8: Streaming Backpressure for Indexing","description":"## Overview
Stream per-connector data with bounded channel to single ingest worker, reducing peak memory usage.

## Background (from PLAN Section 8)
Current behavior:
```rust
// Collect ALL pending_batches across ALL connectors BEFORE ingesting
let all_batches: Vec<Batch> = connectors
    .iter()
    .flat_map(|c| c.pending_batches())
    .collect();  // Memory spike!

for batch in all_batches {
    ingest(batch)?;
}
```

**Problem**: For large corpora (10k+ conversations), collecting all batches before processing causes:
- Peak RSS spike (295 MB observed)
- Memory pressure during batch collection
- Delayed processing start

## Implementation Strategy
Stream with bounded channel for backpressure:
```rust
use crossbeam_channel::{bounded, Sender, Receiver};

const CHANNEL_SIZE: usize = 32;  // Backpressure threshold

fn index_streaming(connectors: Vec<Connector>) -> Result<()> {
    let (tx, rx): (Sender<Batch>, Receiver<Batch>) = bounded(CHANNEL_SIZE);
    
    // Producer threads (per connector)
    let handles: Vec<_> = connectors
        .into_iter()
        .map(|c| {
            let tx = tx.clone();
            std::thread::spawn(move || {
                for batch in c.pending_batches() {
                    tx.send(batch).ok();  // Blocks when channel full
                }
            })
        })
        .collect();
    
    drop(tx);  // Close sender side
    
    // Consumer (single ingest worker)
    for batch in rx {
        ingest(batch)?;
    }
    
    for h in handles { h.join().ok(); }
    Ok(())
}
```

## Technical Considerations
- **Ordering**: Batch order may differ from current implementation
- **Tie-breaking**: Must ensure deterministic final index state
- **Backpressure**: Channel size controls memory ceiling
- **Error handling**: Producer errors must propagate cleanly

## Risk Assessment
- **MEDIUM RISK**: Ordering/tie-breaking could change if ingestion becomes interleaved differently
- Must verify with metamorphic tests

## Equivalence Oracle
Metamorphic tests: indexing in "batch" vs "stream" mode yields identical search results:
```rust
∀ corpus: search(index_batch(corpus)) ≡ search(index_stream(corpus))
```

## Rollback
Feature flag `CASS_STREAMING_INDEX=0` for quick revert to batch mode.

## Expected Impact
- Peak RSS reduction (295 MB → ~150 MB estimated)
- More consistent memory usage during indexing
- Slightly higher CPU due to channel overhead

## Dependencies

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-ug9z","title":"Opt 2.5: Quickselect for Top-K Selection (5-10% faster)","description":"# Optimization 2.5: Quickselect for Top-K Selection (5-10% faster)

## Summary
Top-K result selection currently sorts all results then takes K. For K << N,
quickselect/introselect provides O(N) average vs O(N log N) for full sort.

## Location
- **File:** src/search/query.rs
- **Lines:** Result ranking and selection, rrf_fuse_hits function
- **Related:** RRF fusion, final result selection

## Current Implementation
\`\`\`rust
fn top_k(results: Vec<SearchResult>, k: usize) -> Vec<SearchResult> {
    let mut sorted = results;
    sorted.sort_by(|a, b| b.score.partial_cmp(&a.score).unwrap());
    sorted.truncate(k);
    sorted
}
\`\`\`

## Problem Analysis
1. **Full sort:** O(N log N) even when K is small
2. **Typical case:** k=20-50, N=1000-10000
3. **Wasted work:** Precise ordering beyond top K is unnecessary
4. **NaN handling:** partial_cmp().unwrap() panics on NaN scores

## Proposed Solution
\`\`\`rust
use std::cmp::Ordering;

/// Compare floats with NaN handling (NaN sorts to end)
fn cmp_score_desc(a: &SearchResult, b: &SearchResult) -> Ordering {
    match (a.score.is_nan(), b.score.is_nan()) {
        (true, true) => Ordering::Equal,
        (true, false) => Ordering::Greater,  // NaN after real numbers
        (false, true) => Ordering::Less,
        (false, false) => b.score.partial_cmp(&a.score).unwrap(),
    }
}

fn top_k_quickselect(mut results: Vec<SearchResult>, k: usize) -> Vec<SearchResult> {
    let n = results.len();
    
    // Edge cases
    if n == 0 {
        return results;
    }
    if n <= k {
        results.sort_by(cmp_score_desc);
        return results;
    }
    
    // Threshold: for small N, full sort is faster due to overhead
    const QUICKSELECT_THRESHOLD: usize = 64;
    if n < QUICKSELECT_THRESHOLD {
        results.sort_by(cmp_score_desc);
        results.truncate(k);
        return results;
    }
    
    // Partition to find top K (unordered) in O(N)
    results.select_nth_unstable_by(k - 1, cmp_score_desc);
    
    // Truncate to top K, then sort just those in O(K log K)
    results.truncate(k);
    results.sort_by(cmp_score_desc);
    
    results
}
\`\`\`

## Why This Works
- **select_nth_unstable_by:** O(N) average to partition around Kth element
- **truncate:** O(1) to discard elements beyond K
- **Final sort:** O(K log K) to order just the top K
- **Total:** O(N + K log K) vs O(N log N) for full sort
- **Savings:** When K=25, N=10000: ~3x faster

## Implementation Steps
1. [ ] Add NaN-safe score comparison function
2. [ ] Implement quickselect-based top_k
3. [ ] Add threshold tuning based on benchmarks
4. [ ] Update rrf_fuse_hits to use new function
5. [ ] Verify result ordering is correct
6. [ ] Add comprehensive benchmarks

## Comprehensive Testing Strategy

### Unit Tests
\`\`\`rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;
    
    fn make_result(id: &str, score: f32) -> SearchResult {
        SearchResult {
            id: id.to_string(),
            score,
            ..Default::default()
        }
    }
    
    /// Basic top-K selection
    #[test]
    fn test_top_k_basic() {
        let results = vec![
            make_result("a", 1.0),
            make_result("b", 3.0),
            make_result("c", 2.0),
            make_result("d", 5.0),
            make_result("e", 4.0),
        ];
        
        let top = top_k_quickselect(results, 3);
        
        assert_eq!(top.len(), 3);
        assert_eq!(top[0].id, "d"); // 5.0
        assert_eq!(top[1].id, "e"); // 4.0
        assert_eq!(top[2].id, "b"); // 3.0
    }
    
    /// Empty input
    #[test]
    fn test_top_k_empty() {
        let results: Vec<SearchResult> = vec![];
        let top = top_k_quickselect(results, 10);
        assert!(top.is_empty());
    }
    
    /// K larger than N
    #[test]
    fn test_top_k_larger_than_n() {
        let results = vec![
            make_result("a", 1.0),
            make_result("b", 2.0),
        ];
        
        let top = top_k_quickselect(results, 10);
        
        assert_eq!(top.len(), 2);
        assert_eq!(top[0].id, "b"); // 2.0
        assert_eq!(top[1].id, "a"); // 1.0
    }
    
    /// K equals N
    #[test]
    fn test_top_k_equals_n() {
        let results = vec![
            make_result("a", 3.0),
            make_result("b", 1.0),
            make_result("c", 2.0),
        ];
        
        let top = top_k_quickselect(results, 3);
        
        assert_eq!(top.len(), 3);
        assert_eq!(top[0].id, "a");
        assert_eq!(top[1].id, "c");
        assert_eq!(top[2].id, "b");
    }
    
    /// K = 1
    #[test]
    fn test_top_k_one() {
        let results = vec![
            make_result("a", 1.0),
            make_result("b", 3.0),
            make_result("c", 2.0),
        ];
        
        let top = top_k_quickselect(results, 1);
        
        assert_eq!(top.len(), 1);
        assert_eq!(top[0].id, "b");
        assert_eq!(top[0].score, 3.0);
    }
    
    /// NaN score handling
    #[test]
    fn test_top_k_nan_scores() {
        let results = vec![
            make_result("a", f32::NAN),
            make_result("b", 3.0),
            make_result("c", f32::NAN),
            make_result("d", 5.0),
            make_result("e", 1.0),
        ];
        
        let top = top_k_quickselect(results, 3);
        
        // NaN should be sorted to end, so top 3 are real numbers
        assert_eq!(top.len(), 3);
        assert!(!top[0].score.is_nan());
        assert!(!top[1].score.is_nan());
        assert!(!top[2].score.is_nan());
        
        // Should be 5.0, 3.0, 1.0
        assert_eq!(top[0].score, 5.0);
        assert_eq!(top[1].score, 3.0);
        assert_eq!(top[2].score, 1.0);
    }
    
    /// Negative scores
    #[test]
    fn test_top_k_negative_scores() {
        let results = vec![
            make_result("a", -1.0),
            make_result("b", -3.0),
            make_result("c", 0.0),
            make_result("d", -0.5),
        ];
        
        let top = top_k_quickselect(results, 2);
        
        assert_eq!(top.len(), 2);
        assert_eq!(top[0].id, "c"); // 0.0
        assert_eq!(top[1].id, "d"); // -0.5
    }
    
    /// Duplicate scores
    #[test]
    fn test_top_k_duplicate_scores() {
        let results = vec![
            make_result("a", 2.0),
            make_result("b", 2.0),
            make_result("c", 2.0),
            make_result("d", 1.0),
        ];
        
        let top = top_k_quickselect(results, 2);
        
        assert_eq!(top.len(), 2);
        assert_eq!(top[0].score, 2.0);
        assert_eq!(top[1].score, 2.0);
    }
}
\`\`\`

### Equivalence Tests with Baseline
\`\`\`rust
/// Verify quickselect returns same results as full sort
#[test]
fn test_top_k_equivalence() {
    let mut rng = rand::thread_rng();
    
    for _ in 0..100 {
        let n = rng.gen_range(1..1000);
        let k = rng.gen_range(1..=n);
        
        let results: Vec<SearchResult> = (0..n)
            .map(|i| make_result(&format!("r{}", i), rng.gen()))
            .collect();
        
        let mut baseline = results.clone();
        baseline.sort_by(cmp_score_desc);
        baseline.truncate(k);
        
        let quickselect = top_k_quickselect(results, k);
        
        // Same length
        assert_eq!(quickselect.len(), baseline.len());
        
        // Same elements in same order
        for (q, b) in quickselect.iter().zip(baseline.iter()) {
            assert_eq!(q.id, b.id);
            assert_eq!(q.score, b.score);
        }
    }
}
\`\`\`

### Property-Based Tests
\`\`\`rust
use proptest::prelude::*;

proptest! {
    /// Property: result length is min(K, N)
    #[test]
    fn prop_top_k_length(n in 0usize..1000, k in 1usize..100) {
        let results: Vec<SearchResult> = (0..n)
            .map(|i| make_result(&format!("r{}", i), i as f32))
            .collect();
        
        let top = top_k_quickselect(results, k);
        prop_assert_eq!(top.len(), n.min(k));
    }
    
    /// Property: results are sorted in descending order
    #[test]
    fn prop_top_k_sorted(n in 1usize..500, k in 1usize..50) {
        let results: Vec<SearchResult> = (0..n)
            .map(|i| make_result(&format!("r{}", i), (i * 17 % 100) as f32))
            .collect();
        
        let top = top_k_quickselect(results, k);
        
        for w in top.windows(2) {
            prop_assert!(w[0].score >= w[1].score);
        }
    }
    
    /// Property: all results are from original set
    #[test]
    fn prop_top_k_subset(n in 1usize..500, k in 1usize..50) {
        let results: Vec<SearchResult> = (0..n)
            .map(|i| make_result(&format!("r{}", i), i as f32))
            .collect();
        
        let ids: HashSet<_> = results.iter().map(|r| &r.id).collect();
        let top = top_k_quickselect(results, k);
        
        for r in &top {
            prop_assert!(ids.contains(&r.id));
        }
    }
    
    /// Property: no result in top-K has score less than excluded results
    #[test]
    fn prop_top_k_correct_partition(n in 10usize..500, k in 1usize..10) {
        let results: Vec<SearchResult> = (0..n)
            .map(|i| make_result(&format!("r{}", i), (i * 7 % 1000) as f32))
            .collect();
        
        let all_scores: Vec<f32> = results.iter().map(|r| r.score).collect();
        let top = top_k_quickselect(results, k);
        
        if !top.is_empty() {
            let min_top_score = top.iter().map(|r| r.score).fold(f32::INFINITY, f32::min);
            let mut sorted_all = all_scores.clone();
            sorted_all.sort_by(|a, b| b.partial_cmp(a).unwrap());
            
            // The k-th highest score should be <= min score in top-k
            if let Some(&kth) = sorted_all.get(k - 1) {
                prop_assert!(min_top_score >= kth);
            }
        }
    }
}
\`\`\`

### Benchmark Suite
\`\`\`rust
use criterion::{BenchmarkId, Criterion, criterion_group, criterion_main};

fn bench_top_k_scaling(c: &mut Criterion) {
    let mut group = c.benchmark_group("top_k_scaling");
    
    let k = 25;
    for n in [100, 500, 1_000, 5_000, 10_000, 50_000] {
        let results: Vec<SearchResult> = (0..n)
            .map(|i| make_result(&format!("r{}", i), i as f32))
            .collect();
        
        group.bench_with_input(
            BenchmarkId::new("full_sort", n),
            &results,
            |b, results| {
                b.iter_batched(
                    || results.clone(),
                    |mut r| {
                        r.sort_by(cmp_score_desc);
                        r.truncate(k);
                        r
                    },
                    criterion::BatchSize::SmallInput,
                )
            },
        );
        
        group.bench_with_input(
            BenchmarkId::new("quickselect", n),
            &results,
            |b, results| {
                b.iter_batched(
                    || results.clone(),
                    |r| top_k_quickselect(r, k),
                    criterion::BatchSize::SmallInput,
                )
            },
        );
    }
    
    group.finish();
}

fn bench_top_k_varying_k(c: &mut Criterion) {
    let mut group = c.benchmark_group("top_k_varying_k");
    
    let n = 10_000;
    let results: Vec<SearchResult> = (0..n)
        .map(|i| make_result(&format!("r{}", i), i as f32))
        .collect();
    
    for k in [1, 10, 25, 50, 100, 500, 1000] {
        group.bench_with_input(
            BenchmarkId::new("quickselect", k),
            &(results.clone(), k),
            |b, (results, k)| {
                b.iter_batched(
                    || results.clone(),
                    |r| top_k_quickselect(r, *k),
                    criterion::BatchSize::SmallInput,
                )
            },
        );
    }
    
    group.finish();
}
\`\`\`

### E2E Integration Test
\`\`\`rust
/// Integration with RRF fusion
#[test]
fn test_top_k_with_rrf_fusion() {
    let lexical: Vec<SearchHit> = (0..100)
        .map(|i| make_search_hit(&format!("L{}", i), 100.0 - i as f32))
        .collect();
    
    let semantic: Vec<SearchHit> = (0..100)
        .map(|i| make_search_hit(&format!("S{}", i), 1.0 - 0.01 * i as f32))
        .collect();
    
    // Use the actual rrf_fuse_hits function
    let fused = rrf_fuse_hits(&lexical, &semantic, 25, 0);
    
    // Verify results
    assert_eq!(fused.len(), 25);
    
    // Results should be sorted by fused score
    for w in fused.windows(2) {
        assert!(w[0].score >= w[1].score);
    }
}
\`\`\`

## Logging and Observability
\`\`\`rust
fn top_k_quickselect(mut results: Vec<SearchResult>, k: usize) -> Vec<SearchResult> {
    let n = results.len();
    
    tracing::trace!(
        input_size = n,
        requested_k = k,
        "top_k_quickselect called"
    );
    
    if n == 0 {
        return results;
    }
    if n <= k {
        results.sort_by(cmp_score_desc);
        return results;
    }
    
    let use_full_sort = n < QUICKSELECT_THRESHOLD;
    
    if use_full_sort {
        tracing::trace!(threshold = QUICKSELECT_THRESHOLD, "Using full sort (below threshold)");
        results.sort_by(cmp_score_desc);
        results.truncate(k);
    } else {
        tracing::trace!("Using quickselect partition");
        results.select_nth_unstable_by(k - 1, cmp_score_desc);
        results.truncate(k);
        results.sort_by(cmp_score_desc);
    }
    
    results
}
\`\`\`

## Success Criteria
- 5-10% improvement when K << N (K=25, N>1000)
- No regression for small N (threshold handles this)
- Identical results to full sort (verified by equivalence tests)
- Correct handling of NaN scores
- Correct handling of duplicate scores

## Considerations
- **select_nth_unstable_by:** O(N) average, O(N²) worst case (rare)
- **Threshold:** If N < 64, just sort (overhead)
- **NaN handling:** NaN scores sorted to end, not panicking
- **Stability:** Results are sorted, but order of equal scores not guaranteed
- **Memory:** In-place, no additional allocation

## Dependencies
- Rust std (select_nth_unstable_by available since Rust 1.49)
- No additional dependencies

## Related Files
- src/search/query.rs (top-k selection)
- src/search/query.rs (rrf_fuse_hits function)
- benches/search_perf.rs (benchmarks)
